classification.summary: This act authorizes the board of council of the town of Franklin, Simpson county, Kentucky, to issue bonds not exceeding ten thousand dollars, with individual bonds of at least one hundred dollars. The bonds are to be payable in three installments (one-third in three years, one-third in six years, and one-third in nine years) and bear seven percent annual interest, payable semi-annually. The bonds are payable to bearer and pass by delivery, with coupons attached for interest. The mayor and clerk are to sign and countersign the bonds and coupons, and their details are to be recorded. The board can dispose of these bonds to pay existing town indebtedness and for town improvements.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Covington
classification.summary: This act amends several sections of the charter of the city of Covington. Section 12 grants the city council power to issue and sell bonds to redeem bridge bonds and pay off floating debt. Section 13 amends a previous act to make the city clerk, rather than the city treasurer, responsible for handling delinquent tax bills and taking the collector's receipt. Section 14 amends a previous act to exempt churches, school-houses, colleges, raw materials held by manufacturers, and machinery in factories from taxation, as well as unsold manufactured goods for one year after manufacture.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns municipal finance, bond issuance, and tax exemptions for specific entities like churches, schools, and manufacturers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 51
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1876 outlines the powers of a city government. It details the authority to erect a work-house, house of correction, and poor-house, and to provide for their regulation and the maintenance of the city poor. It also covers the election and duties of various city officers, including the city attorney, clerk, marshal, collector, treasurer, assessor, warden of the work-house, keeper of the poor-house, keeper of the house of correction, and health officer. The statute further grants the power to fix their fees and compensation, and to provide for the payment of jurors, witnesses, and others for services rendered to the city. Finally, it includes provisions for establishing rules to enforce the faithful discharge of official duties and the penalties for non-observance, as well as the removal of city council members or judges for misconduct and the removal of other city officers at pleasure.

Furthermore, it states that state laws regarding illegal voting will apply to city elections.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 73 1876 .
classification.summary: This statute outlines the election procedures for city officers in Mount Sterling, Kentucky, in 1876. It details the city council's role in judging member qualifications and contested elections, the return and filing of poll-books, the date of the first general election under the charter, the method for determining councilmen's terms by lot, the continuation of present officers until successors are elected, and the qualifications for voters. Voter qualifications include being a male citizen of the United States, twenty-one years of age, a legal voter under Kentucky law, and having resided in the city for one year. It also specifies that a legal voter of the state and a resident of Montgomery county for one year, who owns real estate in fee-simple, is also eligible to vote.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of Glenville, in Adair county, approved March 25, 1872.
classification.summary: This act repeals section twenty-one of chapter seven hundred and twenty-seven of an act incorporating the town of Glenville. It grants the board of trustees of Glenville the exclusive right to tax and license taverns and houses of entertainment within the town. However, tavern keepers remain liable for revenue taxes. Crucially, the board cannot grant a license to sell spirituous, malt, or vinous liquors until the question of such retail sales is submitted to and approved by a majority of the legally qualified voters of the town in a regular election.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details an amendment to the charter of the town of Glenville, focusing on the regulation of liquor licenses. It outlines a process where the town's board of trustees must submit the question of liquor sales to a vote of the qualified residents. This is a local option law, not a Jim Crow law. Jim Crow laws typically enforced racial segregation and discrimination. This statute does not mention race, nor does it establish separate facilities or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The racial indicator is 'none' as race is not mentioned or implied.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the Cleveland Orphan Institution," approved March 17th, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the Cleveland Orphan Institution. It repeals the requirement that the institution be located on the lands of the late John Cleveland and grants the court of chancery for Woodford county the power to decree a sale of the institution's lands within the county. It also allows the proceeds from land sales to be paid to the institution's treasurer for management by the board of trustees. The institution is also granted the power to receive and control children meeting the requirements of John Cleveland's will.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation and operational amendments for the Cleveland Orphan Institution. It addresses the location of the institution, the sale of its lands, and its powers regarding the custody of children. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act authorizes the county courts of Fayette and Scott counties to appoint commissioners to ascertain and re-mark the boundary line between them. These commissioners can employ a surveyor and assistants, and their findings, along with a plat, will be reported to the county courts and recorded, serving as conclusive evidence of the dividing line. The expenses incurred will be shared equally by both counties.

classification.title: Original Construction of Streets, Alleys, & c.
classification.summary: This statute grants the city council the power to open and improve streets, alleys, lanes, roads, avenues, or thoroughfares. These improvements can be initiated by a petition from the owners of a majority of front feet abutting the area to be improved, or by a two-thirds concurrence of the city council members. The cost of these improvements is to be borne exclusively by the owners of the abutting property, apportioned pro rata based on lineal feet. A special tax will be levied, due within sixty days of the work's completion and acceptance. A lien will be placed on the real estate from the time the contract is awarded until payment is made.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Owingsville, Bath County. It modifies residency requirements for elected or appointed officials, restricts the granting of licenses to vend spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors during their term of office, and adjusts provisions related to council votes for liquor licenses and tax assessments. It also expands the definition of taxable property to include money, notes, and choses in action.

classification.summary: This act grants cemetery boards of trustees broad powers to manage cemetery grounds and buildings, including the authority to arrest individuals violating laws, rules, or regulations. It also outlines procedures for handling damages to graves, tombstones, and cemetery property, and specifies how proceeds from lot sales and other revenues should be used for maintenance, debt payment, and the creation of a perpetual fund for preservation. The text does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.

There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.summary: This act details various municipal powers for the town of Henderson, including the vesting of street titles in trustees, the authority to build public works, and the prohibition of certain activities like shooting fire-arms. It also outlines voter qualifications for town elections, requiring non-residents to possess a recorded deed to real estate within the town limits. The act also confirms the continuation of the 'local option law' regarding liquor sales.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the governance and administration of the town of Henderson, including property rights, public safety regulations, and election procedures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The voter qualification, while restrictive, applies to all non-residents regardless of race. classification.title: Obstruction of streets and sidewalks
classification.summary: This statute outlines regulations for maintaining clear streets, alleys, and sidewalks within a town. It details procedures for property owners to remove obstructions, penalties for leaving items that impede passage for more than twenty-four hours, and fines for hitching or driving across sidewalks, or feeding cattle on sidewalks. It also grants trustees the authority to permit temporary placement of building materials and to change street/alley locations under specific conditions. Additionally, it addresses licensing for ten-pin alleys and billiard tables.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on public order and the maintenance of public spaces, specifically streets and sidewalks. It establishes rules for the removal of obstructions, penalties for violations, and regulations for certain businesses. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The regulations appear to apply universally to all residents and property owners within the town. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 111 of the General Statutes, Title 'Towns,' and to Amend Chapter 100 of the General Statutes, Title 'Incorporated Towns,' so as to give Trustees of Towns Power to Regulate Certain Businesses and Animals.
classification.summary: This act grants town trustees the power to regulate various local matters, including the operation of billiard tables and ten-pin alleys, the sale and storage of flammable materials like powder, benzine, and coal oil, the maintenance of chimneys and flues, the licensing of grocers and others selling intoxicating liquors, and the taxation or impoundment of animals running at large. It also prohibits trustees from being sureties on official bonds.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the regulatory powers of town trustees in Kentucky in 1876. It covers a range of local governance issues such as business licensing, public safety (fire hazards), and animal control. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The regulations appear to be general in nature, applying to all residents and businesses within the town's jurisdiction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Corydon
classification.summary: This act outlines the election process and qualifications for the police judge of the town of Corydon, Kentucky. It specifies the election date, term length, age and residency requirements, and disqualifications for holding the office, such as being a vendor of spirituous liquors or a keeper of a billiard saloon or bowling alley. It also details the jurisdiction of the police judge in town ordinance violations and criminal/penal offenses within Henderson County, as well as civil cases.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the election and duties of a police judge in the town of Corydon, Kentucky. It sets qualifications and disqualifications for the office, such as age, residency, and not being a vendor of liquor or owner of a billiard saloon/bowling alley. It also defines the judge's jurisdiction over town ordinances and criminal/civil cases. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Corydon
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties and powers of the town marshal in Corydon, Kentucky. It details responsibilities related to tax collection, property sales, and enforcing town by-laws and ordinances. The marshal has the power to arrest individuals violating town rules and can call upon white male inhabitants for aid, with penalties for refusal. A deputy marshal can be appointed, residing in town, for whom the marshal is responsible. The statute also specifies procedures for selling real estate to cover town taxes and the redemption rights of property owners.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter and reduce into one the several acts concerning the town of Sharpsburg, Bath county.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Sharpsburg, Bath County. It defines the town's boundaries, establishes a board of five trustees to manage its affairs, and outlines the election process for trustees and other town officials. Eligibility for trustees requires freehold estate ownership of at least $500, age 21, and one year residency. Voters must be male, over 21, residents for 60 days, and have paid all fines and taxes assessed by the trustees. All elected officials serve one-year terms.

# LAWS OF KENTUCKY. 205

restraining orders, and writs of *ne exeat* and *habeas corpus*, and to issue warrants; and it shall be the duty of said police judge to keep a record of his proceedings, a copy of which shall be evidence, and shall have the same effect as records of justices of the peace. He shall have power to issue summons for witnesses to give evidence in causes pending before him, or to give their depositions; and, upon their failure to attend, to issue compulsory process to cause their attendance. He shall have power to fine and imprison for contempt: *Provided, however*, The fine shall not exceed five dollars, nor the imprisonment eight hours. He shall have power to order the marshal to summon a jury in any case cognizable before him, where a jury would be necessary before a circuit court or a justice of the peace. He shall have power to take depositions and certify the same, to be read as evidence in any cause pending in any courts where the depositions now authorized to be taken by examiners may be read.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of New Columbus , in Owen county .
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of New Columbus, Owen County. It grants the town trustees powers over streets, alleys, and sidewalks, including the ability to levy taxes on property and citizens, tax auction sales, shows, and exhibitions. The trustees can also prohibit drunkenness, riots, breaches of the peace, profane swearing, unlawful assemblies, and indecent exposure, with penalties not exceeding those imposed by general state laws. The act also requires the appointment of a clerk and treasurer.

whereas,itisfurtherrepresentedthat the saidsmall estateis

lunatic,and the grandchildrenof saidmother ; now ,

released and discharged , and the said lunatic and her heirs
are acquittedof all liabilityby reason of said claim of the
State,and saidjudgment ,and the executionsissuingthereon.

stead,and is exempt during her lifefrom allclaim and as-
sessmentfortaxes,eitherState,county,or municipal.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company, allowing it to accept a special amendment from the State of Tennessee. The act outlines the process for reporting and paying this tax, including penalties for failure to comply.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of Stephensport, in Breckinridge county.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Stephensport, Breckinridge County, authorizing citizens to elect a police judge and a marshal. The police judge is granted the same rights and powers as justices of the peace, with exclusive jurisdiction in penal causes within the town, including the power to punish violations of ordinances, drunkenness, and disorderly conduct, with fines not exceeding ten dollars. The marshal will have the rights of constables. Fines collected are to be paid to the town treasurer.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Troublesome Creek Boom Company, in Breathitt county.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Troublesome Creek Boom Company, naming E. C. Strong, Thomas Hagans, G. T. Strong, Anderson Hays, S. H. Combs, and J. E. Price as corporators. The company is empowered to sue and be sued, acquire and dispose of property, and make by-laws. Its primary purpose is to erect and maintain a boom or piers at the mouth of Troublesome Creek in Breathitt County for catching and fastening loose saw-logs and other lumber floated down the creek. The initial capital stock is set at two hundred dollars, with the possibility of increasing it to five thousand dollars, divided into shares of five dollars each.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation of a private company for the purpose of managing lumber on Troublesome Creek. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate Mount Tabor Church and Burial Grounds, in Lewis county.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Mount Tabor Cemetery Company in Lewis County, Kentucky. It grants the company corporate powers, including the ability to acquire and hold land (up to five acres) for use as a cemetery and ornamental grounds. The act specifies that the land shall not be alienated or used for purposes other than burial lots. It also allows the company to receive legacies for the cemetery's improvement and to invest in State stocks or loans, but prohibits banking powers. The company's affairs are to be managed by seven directors elected annually by lot owners.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the city of Louisville, and directory to the board of commissioners of public charities of said city.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the board of commissioners of public charities for the city of Louisville from using or directing the labor of convicts in the city work-house in a manner that competes with the laboring classes outside the work-house. Instead, convicts are to be employed in tasks such as working in stone quarries, preparing curbing for streets, preparing material for macadamizing streets, and other useful labor on public property, ensuring their work does not compete with labor not under the control of the commissioners.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the use of convict labor in Louisville. While it aims to prevent competition with the general labor force, it does not contain any explicit or implicit racial components. The focus is on the economic impact of convict labor rather than racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 1876 .
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and duties of town trustees regarding ordinances, public spaces, streets, sidewalks, and livestock. It grants them authority to enact ordinances for good order and welfare, post them publicly, open and repair streets and alleys, and pave sidewalks. It also details procedures for collecting costs for sidewalk improvements from abutting property owners, including provisions for non-resident owners. Furthermore, it empowers trustees to prevent livestock from running at large, with penalties for owners and provisions for impounding stray animals. Finally, it mandates that streets be kept clear of obstructions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and regulatory powers of town trustees in Kentucky in 1876. It covers aspects of municipal governance such as ordinance creation, street maintenance, sidewalk construction, and the control of livestock. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The regulations appear to be general in nature, applying to all residents and property owners within the town. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Penalty for failure to pay tax
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for collecting unpaid taxes, including capitation and property taxes. It details the seizure and sale of personal and real property if taxes are not paid upon demand, including provisions for redemption by the tax-payer. It also specifies procedures for sales where the amount exceeds taxes plus ten percent, granting the purchaser a lien on the property.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1876.

The land is to be held exclusively for cemetery use, exempt from taxation and legal processes, and a plat is to be recorded. The congregation can then sell burial lots.
classification.reasoning: The statute grants a specific religious congregation the power to acquire land for a cemetery. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Congregation Adas Israel
classification.summary: This act details the process for purchasing burial lots in a cemetery owned by Congregation Adas Israel. It outlines how lots are conveyed, the powers of the board of trustees to make rules and regulations for the cemetery, and a penalty for violating graves. The statute specifies that lots are for burial purposes only and will revert to the congregation if used otherwise.

It focuses on property rights, governance of the cemetery, and the use of burial lots. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act approved March 8th, 1867, entitled "An Act to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to the town of Greenup," and to amend section thirteen of an act approved February 16th, 1866, in relation to the town of Greenup.
classification.summary: This act amends previous legislation concerning the town of Greenup. It details the appointment and duties of a town assessor, including the process for valuing taxable real estate and collecting poll taxes. It also allows the town trustees to appoint a town collector of revenue and specifies the execution of a bond for this role. Additionally, it sets the schedule for the Greenup police court's civil business and exempts certain lands within Greenup from road tax.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided details administrative and procedural matters for the town of Greenup, including tax assessment, collection, and court operations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it refers to acts approved on specific dates and amends previous acts, which is characteristic of legislative session laws. Confidence is high as the content is clearly administrative and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Zoological Garden and Aquarium Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Zoological Garden and Aquarium Company, outlining its governance, powers, and exemptions. It details the election of trustees, the company's ability to make by-laws, hold fairs for exhibiting animals and fish, and its exemption from taxation. It also specifies that no intoxicating drinks shall be sold on the grounds and that the grounds shall be open free of charge on February 22nd annually. Entertainments are permitted except on Sundays.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 399
classification.summary: This statute details the establishment and jurisdiction of the Mayor's Court of Ashland. It outlines the mayor's authority to hold court, declares it a court of record with a seal, and specifies its exclusive original jurisdiction over city ordinance violations. It also grants concurrent jurisdiction with the circuit court in criminal cases within the city and adjacent areas (excluding felonies), civil cases up to $200, and the powers of two justices of the peace for examining felony charges, committing offenders, and issuing various writs. The court also has jurisdiction in cases of lunacy, idiocy, vagrancy, and the binding out of orphan children or children of disqualified parents. The statute further describes the court's power to issue process and the execution of such process by the city marshal or other law enforcement officers.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes the legal framework for a Mayor's Court in Ashland, Kentucky, in 1876. It details the court's jurisdiction, powers, and procedural aspects. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. classification.title: An Act to Incorporate The Kentucky Infirmary for Women and Children
classification.summary: This act incorporates "The Kentucky Infirmary for Women and Children" in Louisville, Kentucky. The institution is dedicated to the care of lying-in women, treatment of diseases and accidents peculiar to women and children, and the treatment of deformed and maimed children. It also includes a collegiate department for medical instruction and nurse training. The infirmary is to be open to patients from all parts of the world, and citizens of Kentucky unable to pay will receive treatment free of charge. The institution is exempt from taxation and governed by a board of regents.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter and laws of the town of Bellevue, in Campbell county.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter and laws of the town of Bellevue, Campbell County. It specifies that special taxes for street and sidewalk improvements will include all costs and expenses, and these costs will be charged to and apportioned equally upon the front footage of lots or real estate fronting the work. This apportionment will constitute a special tax debt and create a lien on the property. The town clerk will issue tax bills, which will be payable to the town treasurer for twenty days. Delinquent bills will be returned to the clerk.

classification.title: City Attorney and Marshal Duties and Compensation
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties and compensation for a city attorney and a city marshal. The city attorney acts as legal advisor, prosecutes offenders in mayor's court (except felonies), prepares ordinances, and provides written opinions. Their compensation is based on a percentage of fines collected, with different rates depending on the fine amount, and they only receive a portion if they prosecuted the case. The mayor can appoint a temporary attorney if the city attorney is absent. The city marshal is responsible for executing legal processes, making arrests within the county for offenses committed within the city limits, and suppressing disorder. The marshal can arrest offenders with or without a warrant.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the roles and responsibilities of city officials, specifically the city attorney and city marshal. It focuses on legal advisory, prosecution of minor offenses, ordinance preparation, and law enforcement functions. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it does not appear to be a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1876'.

classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1876 outlines the duties of city officers, specifically the city clerk and the collector and treasurer. The collector and treasurer are tasked with collecting and disbursing taxes, receiving and disbursing all city funds, and reporting on financial matters. The street commissioner's duties include keeping streets and alleys clean and enforcing ordinances related to obstructions.

The county court's authority to grant licenses is also mentioned, contingent on trustee approval.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of New Castle
classification.summary: This act details the procedures for collecting fines imposed by the police court of New Castle. It allows for execution against the defendant's estate or a capias pro fine (imprisonment until the fine is paid, not exceeding one day per dollar). Crucially, it grants the police judge discretion to assign defendants to hard labor for the town (streets, public buildings, etc.) at a rate of one dollar per day to satisfy their fines and costs. Defendants are to be under the town marshal's control, with provisions for escape and confinement in the county jail when not working. The act also specifies the court's power to imprison convicted individuals for up to twenty days in the Henry county jail and outlines appeal procedures to the Henry quarterly court.
classification.reasoning: The statute outlines the enforcement mechanisms for fines and punishments within the police court of New Castle, Kentucky. It details options for collecting fines, including imprisonment and, significantly, assigning defendants to hard labor for the town. While this statute involves incarceration and labor, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The provisions apply generally to any defendant convicted in the police court. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text clearly describes a general legal process without any racial distinctions. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1876.

It also allows for the punishment of vagrants and prostitutes and the prevention of obscene exhibitions.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of New Castle
classification.summary: This act details tax exemptions and property regulations for the town of New Castle, Kentucky. It specifies that farms of ten acres or more added to the town will be tax-exempt until laid off into smaller lots for building or sale. Dwelling houses on these farms, along with two acres of surrounding land, will be taxed as town property, and residents will pay poll tax. It also lists various exemptions from town taxation, including public property, educational institutions, churches, cemeteries, and household furniture up to a certain value. Additionally, it outlines the powers of the board of trustees regarding street improvements and the responsibilities of property owners for constructing and maintaining sidewalks.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to the municipal governance and taxation of the town of New Castle, Kentucky. It outlines tax exemptions for land and property, as well as provisions for street and sidewalk improvements. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Greenup and Riverton Building and Loan Association
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Greenup and Riverton Building and Loan Association, outlining its powers, stock structure, officer requirements, and operational commencement. It grants the association perpetual succession, the ability to sue and be sued, contract, hold and sell property, issue stock up to $80,000, and loan funds to its members at legal interest rates, secured by mortgages. Each share is valued at $200 and is considered personal property. The association will have a president, secretary, treasurer, and five directors, all of whom must own at least two shares of stock. An attorney will be elected to represent the association, with the board empowered to hire additional counsel and agents as needed. The association can begin operations once 200 shares are subscribed.
classification.reasoning: The statute clearly defines the incorporation and operational parameters of a building and loan association. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the legislature in 1876.

AN ACT to provide for the payment of expenses incurred by the Ohio
county court in taking care of a pauper idiot.

found to be an idiot,and was,by the proper authority,given
to the custodyof a committee,and afterwardwas given up by
saidcommittee; and whereas,the Ohio circuitcourtby order
directedthe judge of the Ohio county court to act as com-
mittee for said Jacob Hawes ,who had him cared for at the
expense of Ohio county from the 11th of January , 1874,till
the 15th of July,1875, when said pauper died; and whereas,
the Ohio circuit court allowed , and ordered to be certified to
the Auditor for payment ,the claim of said committee from
January 11, 1874,tillJuly 15, 1875, at the rate of seventy-
fivedollarsper annum ; and also forten dollars,the burial
expenses of said Jacob Hawes ; and whereas, the Auditor
declinedpaying saidallowances; therefore,
Be it enactedby theGeneral Assemblyof the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the Auditor of Public Accounts is hereby directed
to draw his warrant on the Treasurer in favor of W. F. Greg .
ory, county judge of Ohio county, forthe sum of one hund-
red and twenty -two dollarsand fiftycents, for the use and
benefitof saidcounty,in payment of saidclaim.
§ 2. This act shall be in force from itspassage .
Approved March 15, 1876.

classification.title: AN ACT to legalize the issue of certain bonds issued by the commissioners of a portion of the Jamestown magisterial district, in Campbell county.
classification.summary: This act legalizes the issuance of additional bonds by commissioners appointed to construct a bridge over Taylor Creek in Campbell County, Kentucky. The original act authorized $38,000 in bonds, but due to unexpected flooding and necessary additional works, an extra $3,300 in bonds were issued. The act empowers the commissioners to seek court validation of these additional bonds, which will then become a lien on real estate in the specified district.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the legalization of bonds issued for public works (bridge construction) in a specific magisterial district. classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the establishment and regulation of common schools in the town of Harrodsburg," approved March 10, 1870.
classification.summary: This act allows the trustees of the town of Harrodsburg to levy and collect a tax on property owned by white persons in the school district to fund common schools. The tax rate is specified, and the process for voting on the tax is outlined, requiring public notice and a poll of qualified white voters. The funds are to be used for common school education in the district.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'qualified white voters' and 'white persons owning property' in the context of levying taxes for common schools. This direct racial distinction in the application of a public service (education funding) is a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation, which enforced racial segregation and discrimination. The statute's focus on 'white voters' for approving a tax that would fund schools, without any mention or provision for non-white residents, clearly indicates a racially discriminatory intent and practice. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the establishment and regulation of graded common schools in the city of Louisville," approved March 22, 1870, and to extend the provisions of said act to other graded common school districts in this Commonwealth.
classification.summary: This act allows for the establishment of graded common schools in districts beyond Louisville. It mandates that trustees keep a record open to inspection by white citizens. Crucially, it requires a separate vote on a two-dollar capitation tax for white male citizens to fund these schools. Furthermore, it explicitly states that trustees must report the number of white children and black children separately. This segregation in reporting and the implicit focus on white citizens for funding and access strongly indicate a Jim Crow-era statute.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white pupils' and 'white citizens' in relation to school admission and record inspection. More significantly, Section 15 requires trustees to 'report the number of white children and black children separately.' This explicit segregation in reporting, combined with the focus on white citizens for funding and admission, clearly marks this as a Jim Crow-era law related to education.

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for the levy and collection of a tax on dogs, and protect the rights of the owners thereof in Fayette and other named counties.
classification.summary: This act levies an annual tax on dogs in specified counties: one dollar per head on male dogs and two dollars per head on bitches over four months old. It requires housekeepers to provide a sworn statement of the number and sex of dogs on their premises to the assessor, and other owners to list their dogs for taxation. Penalties are prescribed for false or fraudulent lists, or failure to furnish them. Assessors are penalized for failing to make a correct list of dogs and are paid five cents for each dog listed. Sheriffs are responsible for collecting the tax under the same liabilities and within the same period as taxes on other personal property, receiving the same commission.

classification.summary: This act amends a previous law from 1873 concerning taxation in a specific magisterial district of Campbell County. The board of supervisors of Campbell County is tasked with examining tax books and levying a tax on real estate within the specified district to cover the bonds issued for these purposes and their interest.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to amend an act approved February 18th, 1860, to empower the county court of Bath county to make subscription to the capital stock of turnpike road companies," approved February 5th, 1868.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act to grant the Bath County Court the authority to subscribe to the capital stock of the Owingsville and McIntire's Ferry Turnpike Road and the road from Owingsville to Frenchburg. It specifies the maximum amount per mile the county can subscribe and requires that private subscriptions, along with county subscriptions, be sufficient to build at least four miles of the road. The county court is also empowered to levy an ad valorem tax to pay for these subscriptions. Once four miles of a road are completed, directors can erect toll gates, with rates not exceeding those fixed by general law, and gates cannot be placed within one mile of Owingsville's limits.

classification.title: AN ACT in aid of colored common schools in Hart county.
classification.summary: This act addresses an error in reporting for colored common schools in Hart County for the school year ending June 30th, 1875. Specifically, two districts numbered 'No. 4' were reported to the commissioner, but one was mistakenly omitted by the Superintendent of Public Instruction, preventing an apportionment. The act aims to rectify this oversight and ensure proper funding for these schools.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored common schools', indicating a racial classification within the education system. While the act itself appears to be a corrective measure to ensure proper funding for these schools, its existence and the explicit racial designation place it within the context of Jim Crow era legislation that often segregated and differentiated educational resources based on race. The confidence is high because of the explicit mention of 'colored'.

classification.title: AN ACT granting to the trustees of the town of Ghent, in Carroll county, the right to establish and maintain a ferry on the Ohio river at said town.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the trustees of the town of Ghent, Carroll County, to establish, maintain, and operate a ferry service on the Ohio River for a period of twenty years. The ferry would connect Ghent to Vevay, Indiana. The trustees have the right to lease this franchise. The act also requires them to obtain approval from the Carroll County Court regarding the number and type of boats, propulsion methods, and number of hands employed, and to enter into a covenant with the Commonwealth to ensure proper operation and to pay any damages resulting from negligence or misconduct.
classification.reasoning: The statute grants a specific town the authority to establish and operate a ferry service. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is solely on municipal governance and infrastructure development. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the city of Lawrenceburg, Indiana, to purchase ferry in Boone county, opposite said city.
classification.summary: This act repeals a portion of the General Statute, chapter 42, title 'Ferries,' that applied to non-resident owners of ferry franchises. It specifically allows the city of Lawrenceburg, Indiana, to purchase, hold, and operate a ferry in Boone County, Kentucky, located opposite Lawrenceburg. The act clarifies that this purchase does not relieve the owners from liability under Kentucky ferry laws, except for the repealed provisions.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation and transfer of a ferry franchise. It allows a city in Indiana to purchase a ferry in Kentucky. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is on the ownership and operation of a commercial ferry service and the legal mechanisms for its transfer. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act incorporates the Dexter and Lowell Turnpike Road Company, granting it perpetual succession, the power to hold property, contract, sue and be sued, and use a common seal. It outlines the company's authority to fix road grades, width, and materials, establish toll gates, set toll rates (not exceeding general law), and use toll proceeds for road completion. The company is also authorized to enter private and public lands for surveying, examining materials, and constructing the road, with provisions for compensating landowners for damages.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Ludlow, in relation to common schools.
classification.summary: This act allows the city council of Ludlow to levy a tax for the support of common schools, not exceeding twenty-five cents on the hundred dollars of property valuation. This tax requires approval by a majority of voters in a general or special election. The act also stipulates that after January 1, 1877, general revenue taxes will not be appropriated for school purposes, except for building and repair of schoolhouses. Fines assessed in justices' courts within Ludlow for offenses committed within the city limits will be paid to the city treasurer for school benefit.

classification.summary: This act outlines regulations for the sale of spirituous or intoxicating liquors by prescription. It requires a written prescription from a licensed physician, detailing the applicant's condition and the prescribed mixture. Prescriptions must be numbered, dated, filed, and recorded in a book for inspection. Physicians must take an oath to only issue prescriptions for sick individuals deemed necessary for their health. Violators face penalties for illegal liquor sales, and the act explicitly states it does not authorize sales to minors. All conflicting laws are repealed.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of Elizabethtown, in Hardin county.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Elizabethtown by repealing a section and establishing new provisions for road work. It makes all persons residing in the town liable to work on roads leading into the town within one half mile of its boundary, unless they pay a $2.50 fee by July 1st. The town trustees are to divide the town into road districts, assign hands to specific roads, and appoint surveyors. The town collector will provide a list of those who failed to pay the road tax, and the clerk will furnish surveyors with lists of assigned hands. Those liable to work who fail to attend when warned will face a fine of $4 to $10, recoverable in the town's police court. The trustees may also compensate supervisors of the assessor's book.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the organization and maintenance of roads within the town of Elizabethtown. It outlines responsibilities for road work, payment in lieu of work, and penalties for non-compliance. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The law applies to all persons residing in the town who are liable to work on roads under general state laws. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 864 .
AN ACT to legalizecertainorders of Hardin county court.
WHEREAS , At the January term, 1876, of the Hardin
county court, the minutes, orders, judgments, and proceed-
ings of the court were not regularlyread and signed before
each adjournment as required by law,and were not read by
the clerkand signed by the judge untilthe 29th day of Janu-
ary , 1876 ; now , therefore,
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyoftheCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That allthe minutes,orders,judgments ,and proceed-
ings of the Hardin county court,made at itsJanuary term,
1876,shallbe,and are hereby declaredto be,as binding and
aseffectualforallpurposesasiftheyhad been regularlyread
by the clerkand signedby the presidingjudgebeforeeach

statuteregulatingthe proceedingsof county courts.
§ 2. This act shall take effectand be in force from and after
its passage .
Approved March 18,1876.

classification.title: AN ACT to change the line between the counties of Whitley and Laurel
classification.summary: This act changes the boundary line between Whitley and Laurel counties in Kentucky. The new line follows the meanders of the Laurel River from William Eaton's to the mouth of the river, then follows the Cumberland River to the mouth of the Rockcastle River, and continues along the Rockcastle River and the Pulaski county line to the Laurel county line. This change effectively moves a portion of Whitley County north of the Laurel River into Laurel County.

This payment is compensation for expenses incurred by Dees in paying guards to assist in controlling Newton George and Fielding Shelton, who were found to be lunatics by the Laurel circuit court and were being transported to the lunatic asylum at Anchorage.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns a specific financial disbursement to an individual for services rendered in transporting individuals deemed lunatics to an asylum. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the county court of Grant county to appropriate money to turnpikes, and for other purposes.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the county court of Grant County to appropriate up to six hundred dollars per mile for turnpikes built in the county. The county's investment is to be held as stock, and the county judge will cast votes based on this stock. The act requires a voter referendum to approve the appropriation, and it will remain in effect for four years after the election if approved.

It outlines the process for authorizing such an appropriation, including a voter referendum. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act mandates that offenders fined in the mayor's court of Cynthiana for violating city ordinances or state laws, who fail to pay or replevy their fines and costs, shall be compelled to labor on public works in the city with a ball and chain until the fines and costs are discharged, at a rate of one dollar per day.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes a form of penal labor for failure to pay fines and costs. While it does not explicitly mention race, this type of 'debtor's labor' or 'chain gang' system was a common enforcement mechanism used disproportionately against Black individuals during the Jim Crow era to extract labor and maintain social control. Therefore, it is classified as an implicit Jim Crow law related to labor. The confidence is high due to the historical context of such laws.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the city of Hopkinsville by repealing section fourteen of article three of a previous act and enacting a new section. The new section defines who is legally entitled to vote for the judge of the Hopkinsville city court and for the marshal of the city of Hopkinsville. It specifies that only bona fide male residents who are lawfully competent to vote for county judge and sheriff of Christian county are eligible. It also outlines further qualifications for voting in city council elections, including age, citizenship, residency within the city and ward, and not owing any city taxes from previous years.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns amendments to the city charter of Hopkinsville, specifically regarding voter qualifications for city elections. It details requirements related to residency, age, citizenship, and tax status. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against a specific racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text clearly defines the qualifications without any racial component. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The category is null because it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Amend and Re-enact the Charter of the City of Hopkinsville," Approved March 6, 1871, and to Amend Article Five of Said Act.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the city of Hopkinsville. It modifies the eligibility and term limits for the city marshal, requires the auditor and treasurer to present a fiscal statement annually, sets the election date for councilmen, establishes property ownership as a qualification for councilmen, grants the board of councilmen the power to appoint a street commissioner, and defines the compensation for the city marshal or tax collector.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details administrative and electoral changes for the city of Hopkinsville, Kentucky. It covers aspects like term limits for officials, fiscal reporting, election schedules, and eligibility requirements for councilmen. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The eligibility requirement for councilmen (property ownership) is a general qualification and not tied to race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for the improvement of public roads in Hart county.
classification.summary: This act outlines the duties of road surveyors in Hart County, Kentucky, regarding the maintenance of public roads. It specifies requirements for notifying road hands, the duration and conditions for a day's work credit, and the compensation and penalties for surveyors. Surveyors are to be compensated at seventy-five cents per day from fines collected from delinquents.

It details the responsibilities of road surveyors and the conditions for road work. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly focused on road work and lacks any racial component.

classification.summary: This act incorporates the town of New Campbellsburg in Henry County, Kentucky. The act also specifies that existing road laws in the General Statutes will remain in effect in Hart County, except where they conflict with this act.

There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the Murray Agricultural and Mechanical Association," approved March 18, 1876.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Murray Agricultural and Mechanical Association, increasing its capital stock limit and expanding its powers to acquire land, erect buildings, establish fairgrounds, construct tracks, and build warehouses and factories for agricultural products and manufactured goods. It also allows the association to establish business offices and engage in various mechanical industries, provided the total value of its property does not exceed its capital stock. The act explicitly prohibits gambling and violations of liquor laws.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of R. F. Scott, late sheriff of Garrard county.
classification.summary: This act directs the Auditor of Public Accounts to re-list delinquent tax lists for Garrard county with the present sheriff for collection. It also instructs the Auditor to give R. F. Scott, the late sheriff, and his sureties credit for the amount of these delinquent taxes ($409.36) and any damages, thereby exonerating Scott from the Commonwealth's claim on account of these lists. The act addresses a situation where tax lists were given to Scott too late for him to execute them before resigning.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and reduce into one the several acts in reference to the town of Marion.
classification.summary: This act amends and consolidates previous legislation concerning the town of Marion. It extends the corporate limits of the town to encompass a half-mile square, with the center of the present court-house as the center of this square. The inhabitants within these extended boundaries are created a body politic and corporate with perpetual succession and the power to sue and be sued, contract, and be contracted with.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct legislative acts. The first act, concerning the 'Christian Church, of the city of Newport, Kentucky,' establishes it as a body corporate. The second act, titled 'AN ACT to amend and reduce into one the several acts in reference to the town of Marion,' deals with the expansion of the town's boundaries and its corporate status. Neither of these acts contains any language or provisions that suggest racial segregation or discrimination, which are characteristic of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, the statute is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the General Assembly. The racial indicator is 'none' as race is not mentioned or implied. Human review is not needed.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 769 1876 . Other officers may be appointed.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the terms of office for elected and appointed officials, detailing how vacancies are filled and how officers can be removed. It specifies that elected officials serve two-year terms (except police judges who serve four years), and other officers appointed by the board of trustees also serve two-year terms. The board can appoint watchmen, policemen, firemen, surveyors, gaugers, weighers, and inspectors to serve at their pleasure. It also details the process for removing elected officials, requiring cause and a hearing, while appointed officers can be removed by a majority vote of the board. Vacancies are to be filled by election for elected officials and by appointment for appointed officials.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the appointment, election, terms of office, and removal of various local government officials. It does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race or racial segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1876. The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on administrative and procedural matters of local governance without any racial component.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 33 of the General Statutes, Relating to Towns
classification.summary: This act outlines qualifications for holding town office and for voting in town elections. It specifies that any free male citizen of the United States qualified to vote for town officers is eligible to hold office, with exceptions for defaulters to the town. It also details procedures for tie votes, appointment of election officers, and election hours. Qualifications for voters include age, residency in the state, county, and town.

Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence. The category is null because it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Marion, in Crittenden County
classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of the town of Marion the power to open, lay out, extend, change, straighten, or alter streets, alleys, lanes, highways, public squares, or grounds. It outlines the process for acquiring necessary real estate, either through purchase and conveyance or through eminent domain proceedings initiated in the Crittenden County Court. The statute details the legal procedures for such eminent domain cases, including petition filing, defendant notification (actual or constructive), appointment of guardians ad litem for infants, and attorneys for non-resident defendants, all in accordance with the Civil Code of Practice.

It details legal procedures for land acquisition, including eminent domain. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1876 details provisions related to the redemption of real estate sold for taxes or assessments. It outlines the period for redemption (three years), the required payment (20% premium plus subsequent taxes), and procedures for cases involving infants, femecoverts, or lunatics. It also addresses situations where no bids are made for property, leading to it being struck off to the board of trustees, and establishes the evidentiary value of deeds for property sold for taxes. Finally, it mentions an annual statement of receipts and disbursements to be made by the board of trustees.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the Kentucky Mutual Benefit Association of Physicians," approved March 21st, 1871.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the Kentucky Mutual Benefit Association of Physicians. It changes the name of the association from "Kentucky" to "American" and modifies the eligibility criteria for membership. The amended criteria specify that members must be of good health and sound constitution, under sixty years of age. It also includes provisions for wives, children, and family members of physicians (aged 16-55) and medical students, provided they meet health and constitution requirements and intend to practice medicine.

classification.summary: This is an index for the Acts of the Kentucky General Assembly, Volume 2, from 1876. It lists various acts by subject and county, including acts for the benefit of individuals, towns, and organizations, as well as acts to repeal or amend previous legislation. It does not contain the text of any specific statute, but rather references to them.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an index to legislative acts, not the text of a statute itself. The index lists various legislative actions, such as acts for the benefit of individuals, towns, and organizations, and amendments to existing laws. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. Therefore, this document is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence. entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, v. 2, 1876, p. 860

classification.title: An Act to amend section 1 of an act entitled "An act to provide for the punishment of persons who shall unlawfully and maliciously assault or beat any person, or persons, with intent to kill, or to commit robbery, or to commit rape, or to commit arson, or to commit burglary, or to commit larceny, or to commit any felony, approved March 1, 1871," so as to include "assault with intent to commit rape."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning assaults with intent to commit various felonies. Specifically, it adds "assault with intent to commit rape" to the list of offenses punishable under the original act. The original act was approved on March 1, 1871.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an amendment to a criminal law that addresses assault with intent to commit various felonies, including rape. While rape was a crime often associated with racial tensions during the Jim Crow era, this statute itself does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions. It applies to all individuals regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.
